traditional.togaf_reference: TSG Leader's Guide, Ch 9; FC Part 5, Ch 2
traditional.summary: Traditional EA defines how the team is structured using centralised, federated, or function-centric alignment models with integrated management systems.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

The organisational design of the EA function, treating capability establishment as an architecture project with defined alignment models, team structures, and operating models.

Responsibilities

Three alignment models: strategy-centric (EA embedded in strategic planning), function-centric (EA part of functional verticals with consolidation), and IT-centric (EA aligned to IT). Core/extended team structure decomposes into General Business, Foundational, and Purpose capabilities. Integration with enterprise management systems: business strategy, solutions delivery, security, finance, and technology planning.

Skills Required

Organisational design, stakeholder management, governance design, capacity planning, coaching and mentoring, and the ability to articulate EA value through metrics across financial, risk reduction, and benefit realisation categories.

questions.text: How well does your EA organisation model account for AI agent management responsibilities?

levels.1: No consideration of AI agents in EA organisational design; agents deployed without clear ownership
levels.2: Awareness that agent management is needed; informal ownership by individual teams
levels.3: Explicit agent management responsibilities assigned within EA; agent architecture function established as cross-cutting discipline
levels.4: Formal distributed accountability model: central agent standards, domain-level agent performance ownership, enterprise agent registry maintained
levels.5: Fully integrated human-agent organisation design with agent governance capacity modelled and scaled alongside agent deployment growth

questions.text: To what extent does your EA function use AI tools to augment its own operations?

levels.1: EA function uses no AI tools; all architecture work is manual
levels.2: Individual architects use general-purpose AI tools informally
levels.3: Specific EA processes are systematically agent-augmented: repository maintenance, compliance scanning, impact analysis
levels.4: EA function operates as a human-agent team with defined agent roles, governed by the same standards it enforces enterprise-wide
levels.5: Agent-augmented EA practice serves as reference implementation; EA agents continuously improve architecture processes

questions.text: Does your EA team own and maintain an enterprise agent registry?

levels.1: No agent registry exists; unknown how many agents are deployed enterprise-wide
levels.2: Partial inventory exists but is not owned by EA and lacks governance integration
levels.3: EA owns a comprehensive agent registry recording purpose, owner, autonomy level, data access, and governance status
levels.4: Registry is the authoritative source for all agent governance decisions; integrated with the architecture repository
levels.5: Living registry with real-time status, automated updates, dependency mapping, and lifecycle tracking

actions.text: Establish agent architecture as a cross-cutting discipline within the EA team, analogous to security architecture, with responsibility for enterprise-wide agent design standards.
Create an enterprise agent registry owned by the EA team, documenting every deployed agent with its purpose, owner, autonomy level, data access, and governance status.
Develop a capacity model that quantifies per-agent governance workload and projects total EA capacity requirements as agent deployment scales.
Define an agent-augmented EA operating model specifying which EA activities are agent-assisted, what human oversight is required, and how the EA team governs its own agents.

changes.summary: Restructure the EA function as a <mark>human-agent hybrid team</mark> or lose visibility of your fastest-growing workforce.
key_shifts: EA team must own the enterprise agent registry — the single source of truth for every deployed agent
Agent architecture becomes a cross-cutting discipline alongside security and data architecture
Capacity planning must model per-agent governance overhead at realistic growth rates
EA should use agents internally as a reference implementation, not just govern others
what_breaks: Alignment models that assume a purely human workforce cannot account for agent operating behaviours
Capacity planning based on headcount ignores governance workload scaling to hundreds of agents
No integration point exists between agent inventory and existing CMDB or service catalogues
Core/extended team structures with no cross-cutting agent function leave agent design ungoverned
what_survives: Strategy-centric, function-centric, and IT-centric alignment models remain valid organising principles
Core versus extended team decomposition applies — agent specialists join the extended team
Integration with enterprise management systems (strategy, finance, security) is unchanged in intent
The discipline of articulating EA value through metrics still anchors the function's mandate

future_state.summary: The EA function operates as <mark>a governed human-agent team</mark> with an authoritative registry, scaled capacity, and its own agents as proof of concept.
future_state.description: The EA team embeds agent architecture as a permanent cross-cutting discipline and maintains a live enterprise agent registry. EA's own agent-augmented operations serve as the reference implementation for the standards it enforces enterprise-wide.
key_practices: Deploy an enterprise agent registry: every agent tagged with owner, sponsor, model version, and authority level
Add agent architecture as a named cross-cutting function with dedicated accountability within the EA team
Model governance capacity per agent and project agent growth to prevent EA becoming the bottleneck
Run at least two EA processes using agents internally before mandating governance standards to others

traditional.summary: Traditional EA defines Chief Architect, Enterprise Architect, Segment Architect (Business and Technical), and Solution Architect roles with structured responsibilities and KPIs.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

A hierarchy of architecture roles from strategic leadership to solution delivery, each with defined responsibilities, stakeholder relationships, and performance metrics.

Responsibilities

Chief Architect: strategic alignment, architecture landscape leadership, governance and risk oversight. Enterprise Architect: operating model design, architecture practices, principles and policies. Segment Architect: capability assessment, standards and policies within business or technical segments. Solution Architect: solution specification, compatibility assurance, security and privacy.

Skills Required

Progressive proficiency across Application, Architecture, Business, Change, Data, Generic, Legal, and Technology categories, with proficiency levels 1-4 mapped per role in a 71-subcategory skill matrix.

questions.text: Has your organisation defined AI-specific architect roles with clear responsibilities?

levels.1: No AI-specific roles defined; AI responsibilities are ad hoc
levels.2: Informal AI responsibilities assigned to existing architects without formal role definition
levels.3: At least one AI-specific role formally defined (e.g., AI/Agent Architect) with documented responsibilities and skills
levels.4: Multiple AI roles defined and staffed: AI/Agent Architect, Agent Governance Lead, Agent Operations Architect with clear accountability
levels.5: Complete AI role taxonomy integrated into EA career framework with progression paths and performance criteria

questions.text: How well have existing architect role definitions been updated to include agent governance responsibilities?

levels.1: Existing role definitions have not been updated; no mention of agent governance
levels.2: General awareness of agent governance needs but no formal updates to role definitions
levels.3: Role definitions updated: Chief Architect includes agent strategy, Enterprise Architect includes agent operating model, Segment Architect includes delivery model classification
levels.4: Updated roles actively used in hiring, performance management, and development planning with agent governance KPIs
levels.5: Role definitions continuously evolve based on agent maturity progression; agent governance responsibilities adapt to enterprise agent scale

actions.text: Define the AI/Agent Architect role with responsibilities for agent design patterns, interaction protocols, guardrail standards, and testing frameworks.
Update existing role definitions: add agent strategy to Chief Architect, agent operating model to Enterprise Architect, delivery model classification to Segment Architect.
Expand stakeholder maps for all architecture roles to include AI/ML engineering, data science, AI ethics officers, and workforce representatives.
Staff Agent Governance Lead and Agent Operations Architect roles to cover behavioural compliance and production agent infrastructure respectively.

changes.summary: Every architect role now carries <mark>agent governance accountability</mark> and becomes more T-shaped — broadening from deep specialisation to strategic orchestration.
changes.body_html:
Industry Evidence

Forrester identifies four emerging architect roles: (1) customer/employee-centric value mapper who builds knowledge graphs linking experience to value streams, (2) digital twin strategist simulating architecture options with AI, (3) enterprise knowledge curator governing semantic layers and RAG pipelines, and (4) agentic governance champion managing fleets of AI agents with guardrails and feedback loops.

Gartner predicts that by 2028, 55% of EA teams will act as coordinators of autonomous governance automation, shifting from direct oversight to model curation, agent simulation, and business outcome alignment. Agentic AI in EA tools (Celonis, SAP Signavio, ServiceNow) now automates data validation, capability mapping, and artifact creation — freeing architects for strategy.

Are Domain Architects Still Needed?

Yes — but the role transforms significantly. Domain (Segment) architects are not made obsolete; they become more T-shaped. Rather than narrowing their specialisation, architects must blend deep technical understanding with broader business insight and AI governance capability. The shift is from designing systems to orchestrating intelligent ecosystems. Domain architects classify every capability as human-delivered, augmented, or agent-native, and govern the agent fleet within their domain.

Solution Architects as Orchestrators

Over a 3–5 year horizon, AI agents will handle routine technical tasks: generating architectural diagrams, writing specifications, documenting integration patterns. Solution Architects shift from technical design to AI orchestration, stakeholder management, and strategic advisory. The uniquely human skills — creative problem-solving, navigating complex stakeholder relationships, and customer empathy — become the core differentiators. Technology teams transition from development to architecture teams that validate AI-generated code and ensure compliance.
key_shifts: Solution Architects must design for non-deterministic outputs with explicit guardrail layers
Segment/Domain Architects classify every capability as human-delivered, augmented, or agent-native — the role broadens, not disappears
Chief Architect must articulate agent strategy to the board in business-risk terms
Enterprise Architects become knowledge curators and agentic governance champions (Forrester), governing semantic layers and agent fleets
By 2028, 55% of EA teams will coordinate autonomous governance rather than perform direct oversight (Gartner)
All architect roles become more T-shaped: deep domain knowledge combined with AI governance, business strategy, and stakeholder orchestration
what_breaks: Architect roles focused purely on technical design lose value as AI automates diagrams, specs, and integration patterns
No defined role owns agent behavioural compliance distinct from structural compliance
No mapping connects agent-era roles to platform engineering or MLOps teams already managing runtimes
No transition path exists from embedding responsibilities to staffing dedicated roles
KPIs for all architect roles ignore agent governance effectiveness entirely
Only 1 in 5 AI initiatives achieve ROI (1 in 50 deliver true transformation) — architects must be able to distinguish viable from aspirational
what_survives: The role hierarchy — Chief, Enterprise, Segment, Solution — remains a sound accountability structure
Domain expertise becomes more valuable, not less — architects who understand the business deeply can govern AI agents effectively
Structured responsibilities and stakeholder relationships per role still organise the work
Progressive proficiency levels across skill categories apply unchanged to new AI skill clusters
The principle of role-specific KPIs extends naturally to include agent governance metrics

future_state.summary: Architect roles include <mark>AI/Agent Architect and Agent Governance Lead</mark> with every existing role carrying explicit agent responsibilities and a broader T-shaped skill profile.
future_state.description: All traditional architect roles carry updated agent governance accountabilities in their job descriptions and KPIs. Domain architects remain essential but become orchestrators of intelligent ecosystems rather than purely technical designers. Dedicated AI/Agent Architect and Agent Governance Lead roles handle specialist concerns as agent volume grows. Forrester's four emerging roles — value mapper, digital twin strategist, knowledge curator, agentic governance champion — represent the career evolution path.
key_practices: Update every architect job description with agent responsibilities: strategy, operating model, delivery classification, guardrails
Define the AI/Agent Architect role covering agent design patterns, protocols, guardrail standards, and testing
Staff an Agent Governance Lead responsible for behavioural compliance, escalation policies, and cross-team standards
Expand stakeholder maps for all roles to include AI/ML engineering, data science, and AI ethics
Develop T-shaped career paths that combine deep domain expertise with AI governance, business strategy, and ecosystem orchestration skills
Adopt Forrester's emerging role framework: value mapper, digital twin strategist, knowledge curator, agentic governance champion as evolution targets

traditional.summary: The Architecture Skills Framework defines eight skill categories with 71 subcategories mapped across six architecture roles at four proficiency levels.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

A structured framework for assessing, developing, and managing architecture competencies. Eight categories: Generic, Business, Enterprise Architecture, Data, Application, Technology, Legal, and Change skills.

Responsibilities

Generic skills cover leadership, communication, facilitation, and analytical thinking. Business skills cover strategic planning, industry knowledge, and stakeholder management. Architecture skills cover modelling, building block design, governance, and repository management. Technical skills span data management, application design, and infrastructure.

Skills Required

Four proficiency levels: Level 1 (Background), Level 2 (Aware), Level 3 (Knowledgeable), Level 4 (Expert). AI appears as a single subcategory — "AI and Expert Systems" — within the Application category.

questions.text: Has your skills framework been extended with a dedicated AI skill category containing multiple subcategories?

levels.1: No AI skills in the framework, or a single generic AI line item
levels.2: AI mentioned in general terms; no decomposition into distinct competencies
levels.3: Dedicated AI category with subcategories: AI fundamentals, agent design, prompt engineering, agent governance, responsible AI, and agent testing
levels.4: AI skill subcategories have role-specific proficiency targets actively used in hiring and development planning
levels.5: AI skill requirements continuously updated as technology evolves; proficiency expectations adapt to enterprise agent maturity

questions.text: Have you assessed the AI proficiency gap across your architecture team against required levels?

levels.1: No AI competency assessment has been conducted
levels.2: Informal awareness of AI skill gaps; no structured assessment against defined proficiency targets
levels.3: Formal assessment completed for all architects against role-specific AI proficiency requirements
levels.4: Assessment results drive individual development plans, hiring priorities, and cross-functional mentoring assignments
levels.5: Continuous proficiency tracking with adaptive programmes; proficiency expectations ratchet upward as AI capabilities mature

questions.text: Does your organisation have structured AI competency development programmes for EA practitioners?

levels.1: No AI training programme exists for architects
levels.2: Ad hoc training: individual architects attend external courses or self-study
levels.3: Structured programme covering AI fundamentals, agent design, governance, and cost structures for all architects
levels.4: Differentiated programme with baseline AI literacy for all and deep specialisation tracks; cross-functional mentoring with AI practitioners
levels.5: Continuous learning programme integrated into career development with quarterly proficiency refreshes and experiential learning through sandbox environments

actions.text: Replace the single "AI and Expert Systems" skill line with a full AI category containing subcategories for AI fundamentals, agent design, prompt engineering, agent governance, responsible AI, and agent testing.
Assess every architect's AI proficiency against role-specific targets; expect to find the gap is larger for AI skills than any traditional category.
Establish cross-functional mentoring pairing architects with AI/ML practitioners to build experiential understanding alongside formal training.
Implement quarterly AI skill refreshes to keep pace with monthly technology evolution; treat AI proficiency as a perishable competency requiring active maintenance.

changes.summary: One AI skill line across the framework is <mark>dangerously insufficient</mark> — architects are making agent decisions they are not qualified to make.
key_shifts: Replace the single AI subcategory with a full cluster of eight agent-specific skill areas
Solution Architect AI proficiency must reach Level 3-4 within twelve months
Pair architects with AI engineers for skills transfer; classroom training alone will not close the gap
AI skills budget becomes a recurring operational cost, not a one-off training project
what_breaks: A single AI subcategory across 71 skill areas gives no granularity for hiring or assessment
Static annual training decays within months as model capabilities shift on a monthly cadence
No distinction between prompt engineering for architects versus developers leads to misapplied skills
Proficiency assessments treating AI as one line item cannot surface the gaps causing project failures
what_survives: The eight-category skills framework structure accommodates a new AI category without redesign
Four proficiency levels (Background through Expert) apply directly to new AI subcategories
Role-specific proficiency targets work the same way — just with more rows in the matrix
The principle of structured competency assessment remains essential and becomes more frequent

future_state.summary: A <mark>full AI skill cluster with eight subcategories</mark> drives hiring, assessment, and continuous development across all architect roles.
future_state.description: The architecture skills framework includes a dedicated AI category with subcategories for AI fundamentals, agent design, prompt engineering, guardrails, testing, responsible AI, multi-agent orchestration, and cost management. Proficiency is assessed quarterly through hands-on labs and cross-functional mentoring.
key_practices: Decompose AI skills into eight subcategories with role-specific proficiency targets per architect role
Assess every architect against AI proficiency targets and build development plans to close gaps within twelve months
Establish cross-functional mentoring pairing architects with AI/ML practitioners for experiential learning
Run quarterly hands-on labs with production-representative sandboxes to maintain currency

traditional.summary: The Architecture Capability Maturity Model (ACMM) assesses maturity across nine elements at six levels (0-5), complemented by CMMI frameworks.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Frameworks for evaluating and quantifying organisational maturity in Enterprise Architecture. The ACMM uses nine architecture elements — process, development, business linkage, senior management, operating unit participation, communication, IT security, governance, and IT investment — assessed at Levels 0-5.

Responsibilities

Maturity assessment produces weighted mean scores and per-element breakdowns reported to the CIO. CMMI provides complementary views: Development, Services, and Supplier Management. People CMM addresses workforce capability. Data Management Maturity (DMM) assesses data practices. SCAMPI provides formal appraisal methodology.

Skills Required

Maturity assessment, benchmarking against reference frameworks, continuous improvement programme design, and the ability to translate maturity gaps into actionable investment cases.

questions.text: Does your EA maturity assessment include agent-specific dimensions alongside traditional ones?

levels.1: Maturity assessment covers only traditional dimensions; AI is not assessed
levels.2: Awareness that AI maturity should be assessed; no formal dimensions defined
levels.3: Agent-specific maturity dimensions defined and assessed: agent governance, human-agent collaboration, data readiness as gate
levels.4: Combined maturity dashboard reporting both traditional and agent dimensions; gap between them drives investment priorities
levels.5: Integrated maturity model with continuous agent compliance monitoring and agent-augmented assessment processes

questions.text: How effectively does your maturity model account for data readiness as a prerequisite for agent architecture maturity?

levels.1: Data maturity and agent maturity are not linked; assessed independently if at all
levels.2: Informal recognition that data quality affects agent deployment; no formal dependency
levels.3: Data readiness formally defined as a maturity gate: agent architecture maturity capped by data management maturity
levels.4: Data readiness assessment integrated into agent deployment governance; data quality thresholds enforced before agent production release
levels.5: Continuous data fitness monitoring for all agent-consumed data; data quality improvements driven by agent performance feedback

questions.text: Does your maturity model define what Level 5 looks like for an agent-era EA function?

levels.1: No vision for agent-era EA maturity; Level 5 defined only in traditional terms
levels.2: General aspiration to use AI in EA practice; no structured maturity target
levels.3: Agent-era Level 5 defined: agent-augmented compliance, machine-readable architecture standards, continuous automated governance
levels.4: Maturity progression stages defined from traditional EA through AI-aware, AI-augmented, to agent-native EA practice
levels.5: Organisation actively progressing toward agent-native EA; architecture standards consumed by agents as operating constraints

actions.text: Extend your ACMM with agent-specific assessment criteria for each of the nine architecture elements at every maturity level.
Add agent governance maturity and human-agent collaboration maturity as new dimensions alongside the traditional nine elements.
Implement a data readiness gate: formally require minimum data management maturity before agent architecture maturity can be assessed at Level 3 or above.
Define a four-stage maturity progression — Traditional EA, AI-Aware EA, AI-Augmented EA, Agent-Native EA — with capabilities, skills, and tooling specified per stage.

changes.summary: Your Level 4 maturity score <mark>masks Level 1 agent governance readiness</mark> — report both or mislead the board.
key_shifts: Dual-score maturity assessment: traditional EA and agent-era dimensions reported side by side
Data readiness becomes a hard prerequisite gate — no meaningful agent maturity without quality data
Start with three lightweight dimensions before attempting full ACMM extension
At Level 5, the EA function itself uses agents for compliance checking and documentation
what_breaks: Current maturity models with no agent governance dimension produce dangerously misleading scores
No data readiness gate lets organisations claim agent maturity while feeding agents unreliable data
The full ACMM requires 90+ assessment cells — too heavy to adopt quickly for agent-era dimensions
Level 5 definitions describe traditional excellence without acknowledging agent-augmented practice
what_survives: The nine ACMM architecture elements remain valid — agent criteria extend them, not replace them
Weighted mean scoring and per-element breakdowns still communicate maturity effectively to CIOs
CMMI companion frameworks provide models for agent-specific variants
The discipline of translating maturity gaps into investment cases is unchanged and more critical

future_state.summary: A <mark>dual-score maturity dashboard</mark> exposes the gap between traditional EA strength and agent-era readiness, driving targeted investment.
future_state.description: The maturity model reports traditional and agent-era scores side by side, with data readiness enforced as a prerequisite gate. A four-stage progression — Traditional EA, AI-Aware, AI-Augmented, Agent-Native — gives organisations concrete milestones with defined exit criteria.
key_practices: Extend each ACMM element with agent-specific criteria and add agent governance and human-agent collaboration dimensions
Implement a data readiness gate: cap agent maturity at Level 2 until data management reaches Level 3
Deploy a lightweight three-dimension starter assessment before attempting full extension
Define four-stage progression with explicit exit criteria so organisations know what AI-Aware requires

traditional.togaf_reference: FC Part 5, Ch 4-5 (Architecture Board, Contracts); FC Part 1, Ch 11 (Phase G)
traditional.summary: Traditional EA governs architecture decisions and project delivery but has no framework for managing autonomous operational entities through their lifecycle.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

No direct traditional EA equivalent. The closest concepts are the Architecture Board (governs decisions), Architecture Contracts (define obligations between partners), and Implementation Governance (monitors project delivery). All assume governance objects are passive and that the governance lifecycle ends at operational handover.

Responsibilities

Traditional governance covers architecture compliance, escalation and dispute resolution, and SLA monitoring within Architecture Contracts. ITSM provides the conceptual precedent for operational service management, but neither traditional EA frameworks nor ITSM addresses autonomous, non-deterministic entities.

Skills Required

Architecture governance skills provide a foundation, but agent service management requires new competencies in AI monitoring, non-deterministic performance evaluation, multi-dimensional versioning, and continuous lifecycle management.

questions.text: Does your organisation have a formal agent deployment lifecycle with defined stage gates?

levels.1: No deployment lifecycle; agents are deployed directly by development teams
levels.2: Basic checks exist (security review, access provisioning) but no formal lifecycle process
levels.3: Formal lifecycle with design, build, test, deploy, and operate stages; entry/exit criteria and required approvals defined
levels.4: Standardised lifecycle integrated with agent registry, SLA definition, monitoring setup, and rollback capability
levels.5: Fully automated deployment pipeline with self-service capability, automated testing suites, canary deployments, and continuous compliance validation

questions.text: How does your organisation manage agent versioning and updates?

levels.1: No agent versioning; unknown which model or configuration each agent runs
levels.2: Basic version tracking for some agents; no systematic configuration management
levels.3: Multi-dimensional versioning tracking model version, prompt version, guardrails, and tool access for all production agents
levels.4: Version management integrated with staged rollouts, canary deployments, and rollback capability to any known-good state
levels.5: Automated version governance with A/B testing between agent versions, behavioural regression testing, and provider update impact assessment

questions.text: Does your organisation have agent-specific incident management and retirement processes?

levels.1: No agent-specific incident or retirement processes; generic ITSM applied without adaptation
levels.2: Agent incidents handled ad hoc; no defined containment, triage, or retirement criteria
levels.3: Agent incident management defined with automated containment, reasoning trace analysis, and post-incident guardrail updates; retirement criteria documented
levels.4: Integrated incident and lifecycle management: health scoring triggers proactive intervention; retirement process includes dependency analysis and succession planning
levels.5: Continuous fitness assessment with composite health scores; automated retirement triggers; dependency mapping prevents cascading failures

actions.text: Define a formal agent deployment lifecycle covering design, build, test, deploy, and operate stages with entry/exit criteria and required approvals at each stage.
Implement multi-dimensional agent versioning that tracks model version, prompt version, guardrail definitions, and tool-access permissions as a combined configuration.
Establish agent-specific incident management with automated containment, reasoning trace diagnostics, and post-incident guardrail updates distinct from generic ITSM.
Define agent retirement criteria and implement governed decommissioning including dependency impact analysis, succession planning, and data disposition.

changes.summary: Without <mark>agent service management</mark>, you get orphaned agents burning cloud spend and no one accountable when they fail.
key_shifts: Enterprise agent registry deployed first: owner, sponsor, model version, and cost code per agent
Standardised lifecycle with stage gates for deployment, versioning, monitoring, and retirement
Multi-dimensional versioning tracks model, prompt, guardrails, and tool access together
Agent SLAs cover decision accuracy, escalation rate, and cost-per-outcome — not just uptime
what_breaks: No lifecycle management framework exists for autonomous entities that persist beyond project delivery
No integration pattern connects agent service management to existing ITSM tooling
No business-ownership model assigns outcome accountability alongside technical ownership
Governance that ends at operational handover leaves running agents permanently outside oversight
what_survives: Architecture Board governance of decisions and escalation provides the template for agent governance
Architecture Contracts defining obligations between parties extend to agent SLAs
Implementation Governance patterns apply to agent deployment with adapted checkpoints
ITSM operational concepts (incident, change, problem) provide vocabulary with agent-specific adaptation

future_state.summary: Every agent runs under <mark>governed lifecycle management</mark> with registered ownership, versioned configuration, and defined retirement criteria.
future_state.description: Agent service management integrates with existing ITSM tooling rather than building a parallel operations universe. Every production agent has a registered owner, multi-dimensional version control, outcome-based SLAs, and a defined retirement path.
key_practices: Deploy the agent registry first: tag every agent with technical owner, business sponsor, model version, and cost code
Define a standard agent lifecycle with stage gates for design, build, test, deploy, monitor, version, and retire
Implement multi-dimensional version control tracking model, prompt, guardrails, and tool access as combined config
Establish agent retirement criteria with dependency analysis to prevent orphaned agents and cascading failures

traditional.summary: The Skills Framework treats AI as a single subcategory within the Application category; governance assumes all participants share baseline technology understanding.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

AI appears once in the traditional skills matrix as "Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Expert Systems" with Level 3 proficiency expected for Enterprise Architects and Level 2 for Solution Architects. The governance framework assumes all governance participants have sufficient technical understanding to evaluate proposals.

Responsibilities

The skills framework addresses only architecture roles. No framework exists for the AI literacy that business stakeholders, governance bodies, IT operations staff, and the broader workforce require. Governance board members are assumed to have sufficient understanding to evaluate architecture proposals involving any technology.

Skills Required

Static proficiency levels assessed periodically. No continuous learning mechanism exists for rapidly evolving technology domains. No AI-specific literacy requirements for non-architect roles.

questions.text: What percentage of your EA team has working AI literacy covering model types, agent architectures, and AI cost structures?

levels.1: Less than 10% — AI knowledge limited to one or two individuals
levels.2: 10-30% — Some architects have self-directed AI knowledge; most have surface awareness only
levels.3: 30-60% — Structured training delivered; majority can engage meaningfully with AI architecture decisions
levels.4: 60-90% — AI literacy expected and measured; most architects independently evaluate AI proposals
levels.5: Over 90% — AI literacy is a demonstrated baseline; competency regularly assessed and reinforced through experiential learning

questions.text: Does your organisation extend AI literacy requirements beyond the architecture team to governance bodies and business stakeholders?

levels.1: No — AI literacy is not formally required for anyone
levels.2: AI literacy discussed for architects only; governance bodies and business stakeholders not addressed
levels.3: AI literacy programmes defined for governance board members and business stakeholders with role-appropriate content
levels.4: AI literacy prerequisite gates enforced: stakeholders demonstrate literacy before commissioning agents; board members complete training before reviewing agent proposals
levels.5: Organisation-wide AI literacy baseline assessment conducted semi-annually; heat map of gaps by unit, role, and competency dimension drives investment

questions.text: Does your AI literacy programme include continuous learning mechanisms and experiential components?

levels.1: No AI literacy programme exists
levels.2: One-time training delivered; no refresh mechanism
levels.3: Quarterly literacy refreshes calibrated to AI capability advances; curated knowledge programme translating advances into enterprise implications
levels.4: Experiential learning through sandbox environments with enterprise-relevant scenarios and intentional failure cases
levels.5: Community of practice, governed curriculum with currency reviews, and external engagement ensuring forward-looking awareness

actions.text: Conduct an AI literacy baseline assessment across the full EA team and key stakeholder groups: governance board members, business sponsors, and IT operations.
Implement a structured AI literacy programme with role-appropriate content: AI foundations for architects, decision-making literacy for business stakeholders, operational literacy for IT support.
Establish AI literacy as a prerequisite gate: require demonstrated literacy before stakeholders can commission agents and before governance bodies review agent proposals.
Deploy experiential sandbox environments with enterprise-relevant scenarios and intentional failure cases so participants develop practical understanding of agent capabilities and limitations.

changes.summary: AI literacy is a <mark>perishable enterprise competency</mark> — gate agent deployment on it or accept uninformed decisions at scale.
key_shifts: Mandate AI literacy prerequisites before any stakeholder can commission or review an agent
Solution Architects must reach Level 4 AI proficiency — Level 2 is not safe for agent design
Quarterly capability briefings with hands-on sandboxes replace annual slide decks
Prompt engineering becomes a formal architecture discipline, not an ad-hoc developer skill
what_breaks: No AI literacy requirements exist for governance bodies, so boards rubber-stamp what they cannot evaluate
Annual static training is useless in a domain where model capabilities shift monthly
No production-representative sandbox environments exist for architects to build practical understanding
Treating AI literacy as a one-time project creates a growing gap between capabilities and comprehension
what_survives: The skills framework structure of categories, subcategories, and proficiency levels extends to AI literacy
Role-appropriate proficiency expectations work — boards need different literacy than solution architects
Governance participation prerequisites are the right principle, just underspecified for AI
The practice of assessing competency gaps and building development plans applies unchanged

future_state.summary: AI literacy is an <mark>enforced prerequisite gate</mark> for agent commissioning, governance participation, and architecture sign-off.
future_state.description: Every stakeholder who commissions, governs, or designs agents demonstrates role-appropriate AI literacy before exercising those authorities. Continuous learning through quarterly briefings and sandbox environments keeps literacy current as capabilities evolve.
key_practices: Enforce AI literacy gates: demonstrated competency required before commissioning agents or reviewing proposals
Deploy production-representative sandbox environments with enterprise-relevant scenarios and failure cases
Run quarterly capability briefings calibrated to the latest AI advances with enterprise implications
Budget continuous AI literacy as a recurring operational cost and track via semi-annual heat maps

traditional.summary: The Leaders Guide provides two sample EA capability models — purpose-aligned and domain-aligned — with a multi-year roadmap methodology.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

EA capability models define what the architecture function does, organised by purpose (strategy, portfolio, projects, solution delivery) or by domain (business, data, application, technology, security). The Leaders Guide provides a roadmap methodology covering gap assessment, value mapping, and maturity progression.

Responsibilities

The purpose-aligned model includes Architecture to Support Strategy, Portfolio, Projects, and Solution Delivery plus Foundational and General Business capabilities. The roadmap links EA value drivers (cost reduction, revenue improvement, portfolio rationalisation) to enterprise value. Skills and team capacity management are explicit roadmap dimensions.

Skills Required

Capability modelling, gap analysis, value articulation through value driver diagrams, maturity assessment, and multi-year roadmap development for the EA function itself.

questions.text: Has your EA capability model been restructured to include AI-specific capability clusters?

levels.1: No EA capability model exists, or it has not been updated for AI
levels.2: Awareness that AI capabilities are needed; some informal additions to the model
levels.3: Capability model extended with Agent Governance, AI Platform Governance, and Sovereignty and Compliance clusters; owners assigned
levels.4: All new capability clusters have defined maturity targets, development plans, and traceability to evidence-based findings and recommendations
levels.5: Living capability model with governed evolution, annual review cycles, and continuous alignment with enterprise AI strategy

questions.text: Does your capability model define a maturity progression from traditional EA to agent-native EA practice?

levels.1: No maturity progression defined beyond traditional EA maturity
levels.2: General aspiration to adopt AI in EA practice; no structured progression stages
levels.3: Four-stage progression defined: Traditional EA, AI-Aware EA, AI-Augmented EA, Agent-Native EA with capabilities and skills specified per stage
levels.4: Three-horizon roadmap with dependency-aware sequencing actively guiding capability investment across the progression
levels.5: Organisation actively progressing through stages; reflexive governance ensures the EA function governs its own AI use by the standards it enforces

questions.text: Does your EA value framework include agentic-specific value drivers alongside traditional ones?

levels.1: No EA value framework exists, or it measures only traditional value drivers
levels.2: AI mentioned in value communication but without specific metrics or KPIs
levels.3: Agentic value drivers defined and measured: agent deployment velocity, governance effectiveness, model efficiency, sovereignty assurance
levels.4: Agentic value drivers actively tracked and reported alongside traditional drivers; inform resource allocation decisions
levels.5: Real-time value dashboards demonstrate EA's contribution to safe, efficient, and sovereign AI adoption; EA positioned as enabler not cost centre

actions.text: Extend your EA capability model with three new clusters: Agent Governance, AI Platform Governance, and Sovereignty and Compliance, each with defined capabilities and owners.
Define a four-stage maturity progression from Traditional EA to Agent-Native EA with capabilities, skills, tooling, and governance model specified per stage.
Add agentic-specific value drivers to your EA value framework: agent deployment velocity, governance effectiveness, model efficiency, sovereignty assurance, and risk containment.
Implement capability-to-recommendation traceability so every new capability traces to research findings, recommendations, and framework components it extends.

changes.summary: The EA capability model has <mark>no tier for governing AI agents</mark> — add three capability clusters or govern by accident.
key_shifts: Three new capability clusters: Agent Governance, AI Platform Governance, Sovereignty and Compliance
Sequence delivery by dependency: Agent Governance first, then Platform, then Sovereignty
Four-stage maturity from Traditional EA to Agent-Native with explicit exit criteria per stage
EA value framework adds agent deployment velocity, governance effectiveness, and cost containment
what_breaks: No capability tier exists for governing AI agents or managing model portfolios in current models
No dependency sequencing tells organisations which capabilities to build first
No defined exit criteria separate AI-Aware from AI-Augmented, making progression unmeasurable
Value driver diagrams limited to cost reduction and portfolio rationalisation miss agent-specific value
what_survives: Purpose-aligned and domain-aligned organising principles accommodate new capability clusters naturally
The roadmap methodology — gap assessment, value mapping, maturity progression — applies directly
Skills and team capacity as explicit roadmap dimensions remain essential planning inputs
The discipline of linking EA capabilities to enterprise value drivers is unchanged and more important

future_state.summary: The capability model includes <mark>three agent-era clusters with dependency-sequenced delivery</mark> and a four-stage progression to Agent-Native.
future_state.description: The EA capability model adds Agent Governance, AI Platform Governance, and Sovereignty and Compliance as first-class clusters with defined owners and maturity targets. A four-stage progression gives organisations concrete milestones from current state to agent-native practice.
key_practices: Add Agent Governance, AI Platform Governance, and Sovereignty and Compliance clusters with named owners
Sequence capability investment: agent registry and lifecycle first, then model portfolio, then sovereignty-by-design
Define exit criteria per maturity stage so organisations can verify they have moved from Traditional to AI-Aware
Extend the EA value framework with agentic metrics: deployment velocity, governance effectiveness, model efficiency
